content:
Geoffrey Hinton redefines AI "hallucinations" not as flaws but as a natural feature of intelligence and it should be called "confabulations", mirroring how human memory works. He argues that both artificial and biological minds reconstruct information, not retrieve it perfectly, making confabulation essential to intelligence.

• AI doesn't retrieve data like a database—it constructs responses based on learned patterns, just like the human brain.  
• Human memories are not stored facts but reconstructions, often mixing truth with invented, yet confident, details.  
• The same neural mechanisms enabling creativity and reasoning in AI also produce plausible but incorrect outputs.  
• Confabulation isn't a bug but a necessary part of intelligence—both artificial and biological—enabling adaptability and insight.
